item.title: A Sprawling Middle East War Explodes
item.description: A joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran, followed by Iranian retaliation against U.S. military assets, Israel, and other U.S. allies, has engulfed much of the Middle East in conflict. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of the new war’s initial reverberations in the region.

item.title: Arab States Scramble to Adapt to New Regional War
item.description: Since the United States and Israel began intensive airstrikes against Iran on Feb. 28, Iran has retaliated with missile and drone strikes against multiple Arab countries. Although Iran states it is striking U.S. military targets based in Arab states, it has also hit civilian infrastructure and buildings in Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Oman, and Jordan. This conflict marks a hard stop to a recent warming in relations between Iran and some Gulf Arab states.

item.title: Cold Hard Truths: Arctic Security in a Changing World
item.description: An analysis of Arctic security, Donald Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, Russia’s and China’s interests in the High North, and how NATO and European capitals are responding.

item.title: Lessons from Ukraine for Defending Gulf Airspace from Shaheds
item.description: In early March 2026, U.S. Central Command confirmed the first U.S. fatalities of the Iran conflict, with six American servicemembers killed by a drone strike at Port Shuaiba in Kuwait. The article examines the challenges of air defense against low-cost drones like Shaheds, highlighting the high cost of Patriot interceptors compared to Iranian drones.

item.title: How Will This Energy Shock Play Out?
item.description: Although the US-Israeli war against Iran is unprecedented, previous energy and financial crises over the past half-century offer useful lessons for investors and policymakers. Oil and natural gas producers cannot assume that prices will stay high indefinitely.

item.title: Central Banks Scramble as War Drives Up Inflation Expectations
item.description: Energy shocks rarely remain confined to energy markets, propagating through bond markets, fiscal balances, and inflation expectations. The conflict in Iran is already affecting global markets, with oil prices surging, bond yields rising, and traders rapidly reassessing the outlook for inflation.

item.title: US inflation stayed flat at 2.4% in February before effects of war on Iran kicked in
item.description: US inflation stayed flat at 2.4% in February, according to government data released Wednesday, providing a snapshot of the US economy before it was thrown into a tailspin by the US-Israel conflict with Iran. This data predates the war's effect on prices, with President Trump downplaying the significance of oil price shocks.

item.title: The Wisdom of Europe’s “Great Capitulation”
item.description: The European Union was widely criticized for agreeing to an asymmetrical trade deal with the US last summer. But in doing so, it effectively ensured that the US would continue harming itself by imposing high costs on its own businesses and consumers, while taking the economically sound step of lowering Europe’s residual tariffs.

item.title: Russia Emerges As The Biggest Winner In Middle East War
item.description: Oil markets continue to experience heightened volatility as the Middle East conflict escalates, with oil prices trending north despite President Trump signaling the war is nearing completion. Brent crude was up over 5% to trade at $92.21 per barrel, suggesting Russia could benefit from the instability.

item.title: Oil Prices Surge Despite Record-Breaking Strategic Reserve Release
item.description: Oil prices climbed sharply on Wednesday even after the International Energy Agency announced the largest coordinated release of strategic oil reserves in its history. This underscores the scale of supply concerns stemming from escalating tensions in the Middle East and shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Aramco warns of oil market ‘catastrophe’ unless strait of Hormuz reopens soon
item.description: Saudi Arabian state oil firm Aramco has warned of 'catastrophic consequences' for the world’s oil markets if the US-Israeli war with Iran continues to block shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. While Aramco expects to be able to supply about 70% of its usual crude output, its chief executive warned of 'drastic' consequences for the world economy if the disruption continued.

item.title: Iran Warns Oil Could Hit $200 per Barrel as Hormuz Threat Escalates
item.description: Oil markets are bracing for an even bigger potential price shock, with Iran on Wednesday warning that crude could surge to $200 per barrel if the war involving the U.S. and Israel continues to destabilize the Middle East’s energy corridors. An Iranian military spokesperson urged the world to 'get ready for oil to be $200 a barrel'.

item.title: Europe Faces Jet Fuel Price Surge and Supply Shortages
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz since March 1 has triggered an unprecedented distortion in global jet fuel markets, pushing European aviation fuel prices to historic extremes and exposing the continent’s structural dependence on Middle Eastern supply. This disruption has inverted long-standing price relationships, leaving Europe desperately searching for alternatives.

item.title: China Stockpiles Soften the Blow of the Global Oil Shock
item.description: China, the world’s top crude oil and LNG importer, is not as exposed and vulnerable to energy deliveries from the Middle East as one might think. The country has been amassing crude volumes in storage for months, has worked for years to diversify oil and gas supply sources and routes, and has boosted the share of transport electrification, which has reduced demand for road transportation fuels.

item.title: Brent to trade above $95 for next two months on Iran war, EIA says
item.description: The Energy Information Administration (EIA) forecasts that Brent crude oil prices will remain above $95 per barrel for the next two months, due to the impact of the Iran war on global energy markets. This outlook reflects ongoing supply concerns and price volatility.
